content.problemStatement:
You're given two integers NNN and KKK. Count the number of permutations†^\dagger† PPP of the integers 111 to NNN satisfy the following condition:


There exists **at least one** index iii (1≤i<N(1 \leq i \lt N(1≤i<N) such that Pi+Pi+1=KP_i + P_{i+1} = KPi​+Pi+1​=K.
Since the count can be very large, print it modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.

†^\dagger† A permutation of the integers 111 to NNN is an array of length NNN that contains every integer from 111 to NNN exactly once.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK ― the length of the permutation and the required sum.


Output Format
For each test case, print on a new line a single integer: the number of valid permutations, modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.
constraints: The sum of NNN across all tests won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The valid permutations are [1,2,3],[3,1,2],[2,1,3],[3,2,1][1,2,3], [3,1,2], [2,1,3], [3,2,1][1,2,3],[3,1,2],[2,1,3],[3,2,1].
Test case 666: There are no valid permutations.
